Commons category (P373) == * Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '長野県下伊那郡下條村陽皐', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} [[JA:大山田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q85878781}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Oyamada Shrine |画像 = [[File:大山田神社 鳥居１.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地 = {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}}{{jalink|Shimoina District|Q726999}}{{jalink|Shimojō|Q1203050}}Hisawa 4588 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}、former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |例祭 = Second Sunday of April, second Sunday of October |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano|label=Oyamada Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oyamada Shrine'''（おおやまだじんじゃ）is a shrine located in {{jalink|Shimojō|Q1203050}}, {{jalink|Shimoina District|Q726999}}, {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former rank was {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === * Main sanctuary: {{jalink|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}}（Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto） * Side sanctuary: {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}（Hachiman-sha） * Side sanctuary: Hachirō Myōjin（{{jalink|Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356}}）; before mid-Edo this was {{jalink|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}（Suwa-sha） === History === Oyamada Shrine in Hisawa, Shimojō Village has an unknown founding date, but it is an ancient shrine predating the compilation of the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} in Engi 5 (927), and is presumed to have originated between the late Nara and early Heian periods. There are several theories about its original location, and because primary historical sources are lacking, it cannot be determined with certainty. It is believed to have been moved and then enshrined at its present site. In the early Muromachi period, the Shimajō clan, a branch of the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}, came to {{jalink|Ina District|Q11380911}}. In the Bunmei era they built a castle at Yoshioka in Shimojō Village and made the already-enshrined Hachiman-gū their clan shrine, renaming the place Chinzeino. In Eishō 4 (1507), lord Shimajō Ieuji had shrine buildings for Hachiman-sha and Suwa-sha constructed by temple carpenter Yoshimura Izumi from Kyoto. The Shimajō clan later fell at the end of Tenshō, but local devotion to this shrine remained strong and it was crowded with worshippers. In Keian 2 (1649), {{jalink|Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481}} issued a red-seal grant of 10 koku to "Chinzeino Village Hachiman-gū," and successive shoguns continued issuing renewed red-seal documents through the end of the shogunate. In Genbun 6 (1741), priest Chinzei Kiyoyuki transferred {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} from Suga no Ne-no-jinja and established this as Oyamada Shrine, enshrining him as main deity and Hachiman and Suwa as side deities; this arrangement was approved by Yoshida household official Urabe Kaneyū. In Enkyō 2 (1749), records state Oyamada Shrine buildings were rebuilt by carpenter Suzuki Kurōbei Fujiwara Shigematsu. A surviving ridge tag also states that in Hōreki 8 (1758) the Hachirō Myōjin hall was built. Hachirō Myōjin refers to the spirit shrine of {{jalink|Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo}}, ancestor of the Chinzei priestly family. It is inferred that sometime in the mid-Edo period, due to some change, Hachirō Myōjin replaced Suwa Myōjin in the former Eishō-era Suwa hall. In 1872 (Meiji 5), it became a gōsha. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the Hachiman-sha hall and Suwa-sha hall built in the Eishō era (the latter now changed to Hachirō Myōjin) were designated National Important Cultural Properties. The shrine area is one of Nagano's "100 Natural Areas" and has around 280 species of both southern and northern limit plants, making it botanically valuable. === Precincts === * Torii * Stone steps * Haiden * Sacred trees === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings - In 1912 the Ōjin Tennō-sha and Tametomo-sha were each designated as Important Cultural Properties (then Special Protected Buildings). In 1955 they were consolidated as "Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings".Agency for Cultural Affairs Notice No. 5, 2 February 1955 ** Side sanctuary Ōjin Tennō-sha honden ** Side sanctuary Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo-sha honden === Images === ファイル:大山田神社 鳥居２.jpg|Oyamada Shrine Torii 2, at the top of the long stone stairway ファイル:大山田神社 拝殿.jpg|Oyamada Shrine haiden and covered honden, with temizuya in front ファイル:大山田神社 神木.jpg|Oyamada Shrine sacred tree, giant cedar === Address === 4588 Hisawa, Shimojō, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === {{参照方法|date=December 2020}} * ''Shimoina History, Vol. 4'', Shimoina History Compilation Association, rep. {{jalink|Shiohito Ichimura|Q56253217}}, published by {{jalink|Shinano Education Society|Q11386177}}, 1961. * ''Shinshū Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'', {{jalink|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|Q11386181|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun Co.}}, 1981. === External links === * [https://shimojo-kanko.jp/miru/ooyamadajinjya Oyamada Shrine] - Shimojō Village Tourism Association {{commonscat|Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=長野県}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやまたしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shinano Province]] [[Category:長野県の神社]] [[Category:長野県の旧郷社]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:下條村の建築物]] [[Category:下條村の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]]

Commons category (P373) == * Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '長野県下伊那郡下條村陽皐', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} [[JA:大山田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q85878781}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Oyamada Shrine |画像 = [[File:大山田神社 鳥居１.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地 = {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}}{{jalink|Shimoina District|Q726999}}{{jalink|Shimojō|Q1203050}}Hisawa 4588 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}、former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |例祭 = Second Sunday of April, second Sunday of October |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano|label=Oyamada Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oyamada Shrine'''（おおやまだじんじゃ）is a shrine located in {{jalink|Shimojō|Q1203050}}, {{jalink|Shimoina District|Q726999}}, {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former rank was {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === * Main sanctuary: {{jalink|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}}（Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto） * Side sanctuary: {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}（Hachiman-sha） * Side sanctuary: Hachirō Myōjin（{{jalink|Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356}}）; before mid-Edo this was {{jalink|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}（Suwa-sha） === History === Oyamada Shrine in Hisawa, Shimojō Village has an unknown founding date, but it is an ancient shrine predating the compilation of the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} in Engi 5 (927), and is presumed to have originated between the late Nara and early Heian periods. There are several theories about its original location, and because primary historical sources are lacking, it cannot be determined with certainty. It is believed to have been moved and then enshrined at its present site. In the early Muromachi period, the Shimajō clan, a branch of the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}, came to {{jalink|Ina District|Q11380911}}. In the Bunmei era they built a castle at Yoshioka in Shimojō Village and made the already-enshrined Hachiman-gū their clan shrine, renaming the place Chinzeino. In Eishō 4 (1507), lord Shimajō Ieuji had shrine buildings for Hachiman-sha and Suwa-sha constructed by temple carpenter Yoshimura Izumi from Kyoto. The Shimajō clan later fell at the end of Tenshō, but local devotion to this shrine remained strong and it was crowded with worshippers. In Keian 2 (1649), {{jalink|Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481}} issued a red-seal grant of 10 koku to "Chinzeino Village Hachiman-gū," and successive shoguns continued issuing renewed red-seal documents through the end of the shogunate. In Genbun 6 (1741), priest Chinzei Kiyoyuki transferred {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} from Suga no Ne-no-jinja and established this as Oyamada Shrine, enshrining him as main deity and Hachiman and Suwa as side deities; this arrangement was approved by Yoshida household official Urabe Kaneyū. In Enkyō 2 (1749), records state Oyamada Shrine buildings were rebuilt by carpenter Suzuki Kurōbei Fujiwara Shigematsu. A surviving ridge tag also states that in Hōreki 8 (1758) the Hachirō Myōjin hall was built. Hachirō Myōjin refers to the spirit shrine of {{jalink|Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo}}, ancestor of the Chinzei priestly family. It is inferred that sometime in the mid-Edo period, due to some change, Hachirō Myōjin replaced Suwa Myōjin in the former Eishō-era Suwa hall. In 1872 (Meiji 5), it became a gōsha. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the Hachiman-sha hall and Suwa-sha hall built in the Eishō era (the latter now changed to Hachirō Myōjin) were designated National Important Cultural Properties. The shrine area is one of Nagano's "100 Natural Areas" and has around 280 species of both southern and northern limit plants, making it botanically valuable. === Precincts === * Torii * Stone steps * Haiden * Sacred trees === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings - In 1912 the Ōjin Tennō-sha and Tametomo-sha were each designated as Important Cultural Properties (then Special Protected Buildings). In 1955 they were consolidated as "Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings".Agency for Cultural Affairs Notice No. 5, 2 February 1955 ** Side sanctuary Ōjin Tennō-sha honden ** Side sanctuary Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo-sha honden === Images === ファイル:大山田神社 鳥居２.jpg|Oyamada Shrine Torii 2, at the top of the long stone stairway ファイル:大山田神社 拝殿.jpg|Oyamada Shrine haiden and covered honden, with temizuya in front ファイル:大山田神社 神木.jpg|Oyamada Shrine sacred tree, giant cedar === Address === 4588 Hisawa, Shimojō, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === {{参照方法|date=December 2020}} * ''Shimoina History, Vol. 4'', Shimoina History Compilation Association, rep. {{jalink|Shiohito Ichimura|Q56253217}}, published by {{jalink|Shinano Education Society|Q11386177}}, 1961. * ''Shinshū Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'', {{jalink|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|Q11386181|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun Co.}}, 1981. === External links === * [https://shimojo-kanko.jp/miru/ooyamadajinjya Oyamada Shrine] - Shimojō Village Tourism Association {{commonscat|Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=長野県}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやまたしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shinano Province]] [[Category:長野県の神社]] [[Category:長野県の旧郷社]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:下條村の建築物]] [[Category:下條村の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Commons category (P373) == * Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '長野県下伊那郡下條村陽皐', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} [[JA:大山田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q85878781}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Oyamada Shrine |画像 = [[File:大山田神社 鳥居１.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地 = {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}}{{jalink|Shimoina District|Q726999}}{{jalink|Shimojō|Q1203050}}Hisawa 4588 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}、former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |例祭 = Second Sunday of April, second Sunday of October |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano|label=Oyamada Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oyamada Shrine'''（おおやまだじんじゃ）is a shrine located in {{jalink|Shimojō|Q1203050}}, {{jalink|Shimoina District|Q726999}}, {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former rank was {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === * Main sanctuary: {{jalink|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}}（Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto） * Side sanctuary: {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}（Hachiman-sha） * Side sanctuary: Hachirō Myōjin（{{jalink|Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356}}）; before mid-Edo this was {{jalink|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}}（Suwa-sha） === History === Oyamada Shrine in Hisawa, Shimojō Village has an unknown founding date, but it is an ancient shrine predating the compilation of the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} in Engi 5 (927), and is presumed to have originated between the late Nara and early Heian periods. There are several theories about its original location, and because primary historical sources are lacking, it cannot be determined with certainty. It is believed to have been moved and then enshrined at its present site. In the early Muromachi period, the Shimajō clan, a branch of the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}, came to {{jalink|Ina District|Q11380911}}. In the Bunmei era they built a castle at Yoshioka in Shimojō Village and made the already-enshrined Hachiman-gū their clan shrine, renaming the place Chinzeino. In Eishō 4 (1507), lord Shimajō Ieuji had shrine buildings for Hachiman-sha and Suwa-sha constructed by temple carpenter Yoshimura Izumi from Kyoto. The Shimajō clan later fell at the end of Tenshō, but local devotion to this shrine remained strong and it was crowded with worshippers. In Keian 2 (1649), {{jalink|Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481}} issued a red-seal grant of 10 koku to "Chinzeino Village Hachiman-gū," and successive shoguns continued issuing renewed red-seal documents through the end of the shogunate. In Genbun 6 (1741), priest Chinzei Kiyoyuki transferred {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} from Suga no Ne-no-jinja and established this as Oyamada Shrine, enshrining him as main deity and Hachiman and Suwa as side deities; this arrangement was approved by Yoshida household official Urabe Kaneyū. In Enkyō 2 (1749), records state Oyamada Shrine buildings were rebuilt by carpenter Suzuki Kurōbei Fujiwara Shigematsu. A surviving ridge tag also states that in Hōreki 8 (1758) the Hachirō Myōjin hall was built. Hachirō Myōjin refers to the spirit shrine of {{jalink|Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo}}, ancestor of the Chinzei priestly family. It is inferred that sometime in the mid-Edo period, due to some change, Hachirō Myōjin replaced Suwa Myōjin in the former Eishō-era Suwa hall. In 1872 (Meiji 5), it became a gōsha. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the Hachiman-sha hall and Suwa-sha hall built in the Eishō era (the latter now changed to Hachirō Myōjin) were designated National Important Cultural Properties. The shrine area is one of Nagano's "100 Natural Areas" and has around 280 species of both southern and northern limit plants, making it botanically valuable. === Precincts === * Torii * Stone steps * Haiden * Sacred trees === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings - In 1912 the Ōjin Tennō-sha and Tametomo-sha were each designated as Important Cultural Properties (then Special Protected Buildings). In 1955 they were consolidated as "Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings".Agency for Cultural Affairs Notice No. 5, 2 February 1955 ** Side sanctuary Ōjin Tennō-sha honden ** Side sanctuary Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo-sha honden === Images === ファイル:大山田神社 鳥居２.jpg|Oyamada Shrine Torii 2, at the top of the long stone stairway ファイル:大山田神社 拝殿.jpg|Oyamada Shrine haiden and covered honden, with temizuya in front ファイル:大山田神社 神木.jpg|Oyamada Shrine sacred tree, giant cedar === Address === 4588 Hisawa, Shimojō, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === {{参照方法|date=December 2020}} * ''Shimoina History, Vol. 4'', Shimoina History Compilation Association, rep. {{jalink|Shiohito Ichimura|Q56253217}}, published by {{jalink|Shinano Education Society|Q11386177}}, 1961. * ''Shinshū Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'', {{jalink|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|Q11386181|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun Co.}}, 1981. === External links === * [https://shimojo-kanko.jp/miru/ooyamadajinjya Oyamada Shrine] - Shimojō Village Tourism Association {{commonscat|Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=長野県}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやまたしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shinano Province]] [[Category:長野県の神社]] [[Category:長野県の旧郷社]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:下條村の建築物]] [[Category:下條村の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Commons category (P373) == * Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '長野県下伊那郡下條村陽皐', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} [[JA:大山田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q85878781}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Oyamada Shrine |画像 = [[File:大山田神社 鳥居１.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}{{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}{{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}Hisawa 4588 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} |社格 = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}、former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |例祭 = Second Sunday of April, second Sunday of October |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano|label=Oyamada Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oyamada Shrine'''（おおやまだじんじゃ）is a shrine located in {{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}, {{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}, {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === * Main sanctuary: {{ill||lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}（Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto） * Side sanctuary: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}（Hachiman-sha） * Side sanctuary: Hachirō Myōjin（{{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356}}）; before mid-Edo this was {{ill||lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}（Suwa-sha） === History === Oyamada Shrine in Hisawa, Shimojō Village has an unknown founding date, but it is an ancient shrine predating the compilation of the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}} in Engi 5 (927), and is presumed to have originated between the late Nara and early Heian periods. There are several theories about its original location, and because primary historical sources are lacking, it cannot be determined with certainty. It is believed to have been moved and then enshrined at its present site. In the early Muromachi period, the Shimajō clan, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114}}, came to {{ill|lt=Ina District|Q11380911|WD=Q11380911}}. In the Bunmei era they built a castle at Yoshioka in Shimojō Village and made the already-enshrined Hachiman-gū their clan shrine, renaming the place Chinzeino. In Eishō 4 (1507), lord Shimajō Ieuji had shrine buildings for Hachiman-sha and Suwa-sha constructed by temple carpenter Yoshimura Izumi from Kyoto. The Shimajō clan later fell at the end of Tenshō, but local devotion to this shrine remained strong and it was crowded with worshippers. In Keian 2 (1649), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481}} issued a red-seal grant of 10 koku to "Chinzeino Village Hachiman-gū," and successive shoguns continued issuing renewed red-seal documents through the end of the shogunate. In Genbun 6 (1741), priest Chinzei Kiyoyuki transferred {{ill||lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} from Suga no Ne-no-jinja and established this as Oyamada Shrine, enshrining him as main deity and Hachiman and Suwa as side deities; this arrangement was approved by Yoshida household official Urabe Kaneyū. In Enkyō 2 (1749), records state Oyamada Shrine buildings were rebuilt by carpenter Suzuki Kurōbei Fujiwara Shigematsu. A surviving ridge tag also states that in Hōreki 8 (1758) the Hachirō Myōjin hall was built. Hachirō Myōjin refers to the spirit shrine of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356|lt=Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo}}, ancestor of the Chinzei priestly family. It is inferred that sometime in the mid-Edo period, due to some change, Hachirō Myōjin replaced Suwa Myōjin in the former Eishō-era Suwa hall. In 1872 (Meiji 5), it became a gōsha. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the Hachiman-sha hall and Suwa-sha hall built in the Eishō era (the latter now changed to Hachirō Myōjin) were designated National Important Cultural Properties. The shrine area is one of Nagano's "100 Natural Areas" and has around 280 species of both southern and northern limit plants, making it botanically valuable. === Precincts === * Torii * Stone steps * Haiden * Sacred trees === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings - In 1912 the Ōjin Tennō-sha and Tametomo-sha were each designated as Important Cultural Properties (then Special Protected Buildings). In 1955 they were consolidated as "Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings".Agency for Cultural Affairs Notice No. 5, 2 February 1955 ** Side sanctuary Ōjin Tennō-sha honden ** Side sanctuary Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo-sha honden === Images === ファイル:大山田神社 鳥居２.jpg|Oyamada Shrine Torii 2, at the top of the long stone stairway ファイル:大山田神社 拝殿.jpg|Oyamada Shrine haiden and covered honden, with temizuya in front ファイル:大山田神社 神木.jpg|Oyamada Shrine sacred tree, giant cedar === Address === 4588 Hisawa, Shimojō, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === {{参照方法|date=December 2020}} * ''Shimoina History, Vol. 4'', Shimoina History Compilation Association, rep. {{ill|lt=Shiohito Ichimura|Q56253217|WD=Q56253217}}, published by {{ill|lt=Shinano Education Society|Q11386177|WD=Q11386177}}, 1961. * ''Shinshū Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'', {{ill|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|Q11386181|WD=Q11386181|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun Co.}}, 1981. === External links === * [https://shimojo-kanko.jp/miru/ooyamadajinjya Oyamada Shrine] - Shimojō Village Tourism Association {{commonscat|Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=長野県}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやまたしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shinano Province]] [[Category:長野県の神社]] [[Category:長野県の旧郷社]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:下條村の建築物]] [[Category:下條村の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

[[JA:大山田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q85878781}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Oyamada Shrine |画像 = [[File:大山田神社 鳥居１.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}{{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}{{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}Hisawa 4588 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}、former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |例祭 = Second Sunday of April, second Sunday of October |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano|label=Oyamada Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oyamada Shrine'''（おおやまだじんじゃ）is a shrine located in {{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}, {{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}, {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === * Main sanctuary: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}（Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto） * Side sanctuary: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}（Hachiman-sha） * Side sanctuary: Hachirō Myōjin（{{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356}}）; before mid-Edo this was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}（Suwa-sha） === History === Oyamada Shrine in Hisawa, Shimojō Village has an unknown founding date, but it is an ancient shrine predating the compilation of the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}} in Engi 5 (927), and is presumed to have originated between the late Nara and early Heian periods. There are several theories about its original location, and because primary historical sources are lacking, it cannot be determined with certainty. It is believed to have been moved and then enshrined at its present site. In the early Muromachi period, the Shimajō clan, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114}}, came to {{ill|lt=Ina District|Q11380911|WD=Q11380911}}. In the Bunmei era they built a castle at Yoshioka in Shimojō Village and made the already-enshrined Hachiman-gū their clan shrine, renaming the place Chinzeino. In Eishō 4 (1507), lord Shimajō Ieuji had shrine buildings for Hachiman-sha and Suwa-sha constructed by temple carpenter Yoshimura Izumi from Kyoto. The Shimajō clan later fell at the end of Tenshō, but local devotion to this shrine remained strong and it was crowded with worshippers. In Keian 2 (1649), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481}} issued a red-seal grant of 10 koku to "Chinzeino Village Hachiman-gū," and successive shoguns continued issuing renewed red-seal documents through the end of the shogunate. In Genbun 6 (1741), priest Chinzei Kiyoyuki transferred {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} from Suga no Ne-no-jinja and established this as Oyamada Shrine, enshrining him as main deity and Hachiman and Suwa as side deities; this arrangement was approved by Yoshida household official Urabe Kaneyū. In Enkyō 2 (1749), records state Oyamada Shrine buildings were rebuilt by carpenter Suzuki Kurōbei Fujiwara Shigematsu. A surviving ridge tag also states that in Hōreki 8 (1758) the Hachirō Myōjin hall was built. Hachirō Myōjin refers to the spirit shrine of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356|lt=Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo}}, ancestor of the Chinzei priestly family. It is inferred that sometime in the mid-Edo period, due to some change, Hachirō Myōjin replaced Suwa Myōjin in the former Eishō-era Suwa hall. In 1872 (Meiji 5), it became a gōsha. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the Hachiman-sha hall and Suwa-sha hall built in the Eishō era (the latter now changed to Hachirō Myōjin) were designated National Important Cultural Properties. The shrine area is one of Nagano's "100 Natural Areas" and has around 280 species of both southern and northern limit plants, making it botanically valuable. === Precincts === * Torii * Stone steps * Haiden * Sacred trees === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings - In 1912 the Ōjin Tennō-sha and Tametomo-sha were each designated as Important Cultural Properties (then Special Protected Buildings). In 1955 they were consolidated as "Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings".Agency for Cultural Affairs Notice No. 5, 2 February 1955 ** Side sanctuary Ōjin Tennō-sha honden ** Side sanctuary Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo-sha honden === Images === ファイル:大山田神社 鳥居２.jpg|Oyamada Shrine Torii 2, at the top of the long stone stairway ファイル:大山田神社 拝殿.jpg|Oyamada Shrine haiden and covered honden, with temizuya in front ファイル:大山田神社 神木.jpg|Oyamada Shrine sacred tree, giant cedar === Address === 4588 Hisawa, Shimojō, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === {{参照方法|date=December 2020}} * ''Shimoina History, Vol. 4'', Shimoina History Compilation Association, rep. {{ill|lt=Shiohito Ichimura|Q56253217|WD=Q56253217}}, published by {{ill|lt=Shinano Education Society|Q11386177|WD=Q11386177}}, 1961. * ''Shinshū Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'', {{ill|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|Q11386181|WD=Q11386181|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun Co.}}, 1981. === External links === * [https://shimojo-kanko.jp/miru/ooyamadajinjya Oyamada Shrine] - Shimojō Village Tourism Association {{commonscat|Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=長野県}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやまたしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shinano Province]] [[Category:長野県の神社]] [[Category:長野県の旧郷社]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:下條村の建築物]] [[Category:下條村の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Commons category (P373) == * Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '長野県下伊那郡下條村陽皐', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} [[JA:大山田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q85878781}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Oyamada Shrine |画像 = [[File:大山田神社 鳥居１.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}{{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}{{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}Hisawa 4588 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}、former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |例祭 = Second Sunday of April, second Sunday of October |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano|label=Oyamada Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oyamada Shrine'''（おおやまだじんじゃ）is a shrine located in {{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}, {{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}, {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === * Main sanctuary: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}（Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto） * Side sanctuary: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}（Hachiman-sha） * Side sanctuary: Hachirō Myōjin（{{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356}}）; before mid-Edo this was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}（Suwa-sha） === History === Oyamada Shrine in Hisawa, Shimojō Village has an unknown founding date, but it is an ancient shrine predating the compilation of the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}} in Engi 5 (927), and is presumed to have originated between the late Nara and early Heian periods. There are several theories about its original location, and because primary historical sources are lacking, it cannot be determined with certainty. It is believed to have been moved and then enshrined at its present site. In the early Muromachi period, the Shimajō clan, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114}}, came to {{ill|lt=Ina District|Q11380911|WD=Q11380911}}. In the Bunmei era they built a castle at Yoshioka in Shimojō Village and made the already-enshrined Hachiman-gū their clan shrine, renaming the place Chinzeino. In Eishō 4 (1507), lord Shimajō Ieuji had shrine buildings for Hachiman-sha and Suwa-sha constructed by temple carpenter Yoshimura Izumi from Kyoto. The Shimajō clan later fell at the end of Tenshō, but local devotion to this shrine remained strong and it was crowded with worshippers. In Keian 2 (1649), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481}} issued a red-seal grant of 10 koku to "Chinzeino Village Hachiman-gū," and successive shoguns continued issuing renewed red-seal documents through the end of the shogunate. In Genbun 6 (1741), priest Chinzei Kiyoyuki transferred {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} from Suga no Ne-no-jinja and established this as Oyamada Shrine, enshrining him as main deity and Hachiman and Suwa as side deities; this arrangement was approved by Yoshida household official Urabe Kaneyū. In Enkyō 2 (1749), records state Oyamada Shrine buildings were rebuilt by carpenter Suzuki Kurōbei Fujiwara Shigematsu. A surviving ridge tag also states that in Hōreki 8 (1758) the Hachirō Myōjin hall was built. Hachirō Myōjin refers to the spirit shrine of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356|lt=Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo}}, ancestor of the Chinzei priestly family. It is inferred that sometime in the mid-Edo period, due to some change, Hachirō Myōjin replaced Suwa Myōjin in the former Eishō-era Suwa hall. In 1872 (Meiji 5), it became a gōsha. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the Hachiman-sha hall and Suwa-sha hall built in the Eishō era (the latter now changed to Hachirō Myōjin) were designated National Important Cultural Properties. The shrine area is one of Nagano's "100 Natural Areas" and has around 280 species of both southern and northern limit plants, making it botanically valuable. === Precincts === * Torii * Stone steps * Haiden * Sacred trees === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings - In 1912 the Ōjin Tennō-sha and Tametomo-sha were each designated as Important Cultural Properties (then Special Protected Buildings). In 1955 they were consolidated as "Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings".Agency for Cultural Affairs Notice No. 5, 2 February 1955 ** Side sanctuary Ōjin Tennō-sha honden ** Side sanctuary Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo-sha honden === Images === ファイル:大山田神社 鳥居２.jpg|Oyamada Shrine Torii 2, at the top of the long stone stairway ファイル:大山田神社 拝殿.jpg|Oyamada Shrine haiden and covered honden, with temizuya in front ファイル:大山田神社 神木.jpg|Oyamada Shrine sacred tree, giant cedar === Address === 4588 Hisawa, Shimojō, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === {{参照方法|date=December 2020}} * ''Shimoina History, Vol. 4'', Shimoina History Compilation Association, rep. {{ill|lt=Shiohito Ichimura|Q56253217|WD=Q56253217}}, published by {{ill|lt=Shinano Education Society|Q11386177|WD=Q11386177}}, 1961. * ''Shinshū Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'', {{ill|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|Q11386181|WD=Q11386181|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun Co.}}, 1981. === External links === * [https://shimojo-kanko.jp/miru/ooyamadajinjya Oyamada Shrine] - Shimojō Village Tourism Association {{commonscat|Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=長野県}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやまたしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shinano Province]] [[Category:長野県の神社]] [[Category:長野県の旧郷社]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:下條村の建築物]] [[Category:下條村の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Commons category (P373) == * Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '長野県下伊那郡下條村陽皐', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} [[JA:大山田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q85878781}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Oyamada Shrine |画像 = [[File:大山田神社 鳥居１.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}{{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}{{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}Hisawa 4588 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}、former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |例祭 = Second Sunday of April, second Sunday of October |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano|label=Oyamada Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oyamada Shrine'''（おおやまだじんじゃ）is a shrine located in {{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}, {{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}, {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === * Main sanctuary: {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}}（Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto） * Side sanctuary: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}（Hachiman-sha） * Side sanctuary: Hachirō Myōjin（{{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356}}）; before mid-Edo this was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}（Suwa-sha） === History === Oyamada Shrine in Hisawa, Shimojō Village has an unknown founding date, but it is an ancient shrine predating the compilation of the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}} in Engi 5 (927), and is presumed to have originated between the late Nara and early Heian periods. There are several theories about its original location, and because primary historical sources are lacking, it cannot be determined with certainty. It is believed to have been moved and then enshrined at its present site. In the early Muromachi period, the Shimajō clan, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114}}, came to {{ill|lt=Ina District|Q11380911|WD=Q11380911}}. In the Bunmei era they built a castle at Yoshioka in Shimojō Village and made the already-enshrined Hachiman-gū their clan shrine, renaming the place Chinzeino. In Eishō 4 (1507), lord Shimajō Ieuji had shrine buildings for Hachiman-sha and Suwa-sha constructed by temple carpenter Yoshimura Izumi from Kyoto. The Shimajō clan later fell at the end of Tenshō, but local devotion to this shrine remained strong and it was crowded with worshippers. In Keian 2 (1649), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481}} issued a red-seal grant of 10 koku to "Chinzeino Village Hachiman-gū," and successive shoguns continued issuing renewed red-seal documents through the end of the shogunate. In Genbun 6 (1741), priest Chinzei Kiyoyuki transferred {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} from Suga no Ne-no-jinja and established this as Oyamada Shrine, enshrining him as main deity and Hachiman and Suwa as side deities; this arrangement was approved by Yoshida household official Urabe Kaneyū. In Enkyō 2 (1749), records state Oyamada Shrine buildings were rebuilt by carpenter Suzuki Kurōbei Fujiwara Shigematsu. A surviving ridge tag also states that in Hōreki 8 (1758) the Hachirō Myōjin hall was built. Hachirō Myōjin refers to the spirit shrine of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356|lt=Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo}}, ancestor of the Chinzei priestly family. It is inferred that sometime in the mid-Edo period, due to some change, Hachirō Myōjin replaced Suwa Myōjin in the former Eishō-era Suwa hall. In 1872 (Meiji 5), it became a gōsha. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the Hachiman-sha hall and Suwa-sha hall built in the Eishō era (the latter now changed to Hachirō Myōjin) were designated National Important Cultural Properties. The shrine area is one of Nagano's "100 Natural Areas" and has around 280 species of both southern and northern limit plants, making it botanically valuable. === Precincts === * Torii * Stone steps * Haiden * Sacred trees === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings - In 1912 the Ōjin Tennō-sha and Tametomo-sha were each designated as Important Cultural Properties (then Special Protected Buildings). In 1955 they were consolidated as "Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings".Agency for Cultural Affairs Notice No. 5, 2 February 1955 ** Side sanctuary Ōjin Tennō-sha honden ** Side sanctuary Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo-sha honden === Images === ファイル:大山田神社 鳥居２.jpg|Oyamada Shrine Torii 2, at the top of the long stone stairway ファイル:大山田神社 拝殿.jpg|Oyamada Shrine haiden and covered honden, with temizuya in front ファイル:大山田神社 神木.jpg|Oyamada Shrine sacred tree, giant cedar === Address === 4588 Hisawa, Shimojō, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === {{参照方法|date=December 2020}} * ''Shimoina History, Vol. 4'', Shimoina History Compilation Association, rep. {{ill|lt=Shiohito Ichimura|Q56253217|WD=Q56253217}}, published by {{ill|lt=Shinano Education Society|Q11386177|WD=Q11386177}}, 1961. * ''Shinshū Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'', {{ill|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|Q11386181|WD=Q11386181|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun Co.}}, 1981. === External links === * [https://shimojo-kanko.jp/miru/ooyamadajinjya Oyamada Shrine] - Shimojō Village Tourism Association {{commonscat|Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=長野県}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやまたしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shinano Province]] [[Category:長野県の神社]] [[Category:長野県の旧郷社]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:下條村の建築物]] [[Category:下條村の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Commons category (P373) == * Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '長野県下伊那郡下條村陽皐', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} [[JA:大山田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q85878781}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Oyamada Shrine |画像 = [[File:大山田神社 鳥居１.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}{{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}{{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}Hisawa 4588 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}、former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |例祭 = Second Sunday of April, second Sunday of October |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano|label=Oyamada Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oyamada Shrine'''（おおやまだじんじゃ）is a shrine located in {{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}, {{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}, {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === * Main sanctuary: {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}}（Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto） * Side sanctuary: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}（Hachiman-sha） * Side sanctuary: Hachirō Myōjin（{{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356}}）; before mid-Edo this was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}（Suwa-sha） === History === Oyamada Shrine in Hisawa, Shimojō Village has an unknown founding date, but it is an ancient shrine predating the compilation of the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}} in Engi 5 (927), and is presumed to have originated between the late Nara and early Heian periods. There are several theories about its original location, and because primary historical sources are lacking, it cannot be determined with certainty. It is believed to have been moved and then enshrined at its present site. In the early Muromachi period, the Shimajō clan, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114}}, came to {{ill|lt=Ina District|Q11380911|WD=Q11380911}}. In the Bunmei era they built a castle at Yoshioka in Shimojō Village and made the already-enshrined Hachiman-gū their clan shrine, renaming the place Chinzeino. In Eishō 4 (1507), lord Shimajō Ieuji had shrine buildings for Hachiman-sha and Suwa-sha constructed by temple carpenter Yoshimura Izumi from Kyoto. The Shimajō clan later fell at the end of Tenshō, but local devotion to this shrine remained strong and it was crowded with worshippers. In Keian 2 (1649), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481}} issued a red-seal grant of 10 koku to "Chinzeino Village Hachiman-gū," and successive shoguns continued issuing renewed red-seal documents through the end of the shogunate. In Genbun 6 (1741), priest Chinzei Kiyoyuki transferred {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} from Suga no Ne-no-jinja and established this as Oyamada Shrine, enshrining him as main deity and Hachiman and Suwa as side deities; this arrangement was approved by Yoshida household official Urabe Kaneyū. In Enkyō 2 (1749), records state Oyamada Shrine buildings were rebuilt by carpenter Suzuki Kurōbei Fujiwara Shigematsu. A surviving ridge tag also states that in Hōreki 8 (1758) the Hachirō Myōjin hall was built. Hachirō Myōjin refers to the spirit shrine of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356|lt=Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo}}, ancestor of the Chinzei priestly family. It is inferred that sometime in the mid-Edo period, due to some change, Hachirō Myōjin replaced Suwa Myōjin in the former Eishō-era Suwa hall. In 1872 (Meiji 5), it became a gōsha. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the Hachiman-sha hall and Suwa-sha hall built in the Eishō era (the latter now changed to Hachirō Myōjin) were designated National Important Cultural Properties. The shrine area is one of Nagano's "100 Natural Areas" and has around 280 species of both southern and northern limit plants, making it botanically valuable. === Precincts === * Torii * Stone steps * Haiden * Sacred trees === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings - In 1912 the Ōjin Tennō-sha and Tametomo-sha were each designated as Important Cultural Properties (then Special Protected Buildings). In 1955 they were consolidated as "Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings".Agency for Cultural Affairs Notice No. 5, 2 February 1955 ** Side sanctuary Ōjin Tennō-sha honden ** Side sanctuary Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo-sha honden === Images === ファイル:大山田神社 鳥居２.jpg|Oyamada Shrine Torii 2, at the top of the long stone stairway ファイル:大山田神社 拝殿.jpg|Oyamada Shrine haiden and covered honden, with temizuya in front ファイル:大山田神社 神木.jpg|Oyamada Shrine sacred tree, giant cedar === Address === 4588 Hisawa, Shimojō, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === {{参照方法|date=December 2020}} * ''Shimoina History, Vol. 4'', Shimoina History Compilation Association, rep. {{ill|lt=Shiohito Ichimura|Q56253217|WD=Q56253217}}, published by {{ill|lt=Shinano Education Society|Q11386177|WD=Q11386177}}, 1961. * ''Shinshū Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'', {{ill|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|Q11386181|WD=Q11386181|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun Co.}}, 1981. === External links === * [https://shimojo-kanko.jp/miru/ooyamadajinjya Oyamada Shrine] - Shimojō Village Tourism Association {{commonscat|Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=長野県}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやまたしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shinano Province]] [[Category:長野県の神社]] [[Category:長野県の旧郷社]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:下條村の建築物]] [[Category:下條村の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Commons category (P373) == * Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '長野県下伊那郡下條村陽皐', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} [[JA:大山田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q85878781}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Oyamada Shrine |画像 = [[File:大山田神社 鳥居１.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}{{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}{{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}Hisawa 4588 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}、former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |例祭 = Second Sunday of April, second Sunday of October |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano|label=Oyamada Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oyamada Shrine'''（おおやまだじんじゃ）is a shrine located in {{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}, {{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}, {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === * Main sanctuary: {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}}（Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto） * Side sanctuary: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}（Hachiman-sha） * Side sanctuary: Hachirō Myōjin（{{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356}}）; before mid-Edo this was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=q|WD=Q9150391}}（Suwa-sha） === History === Oyamada Shrine in Hisawa, Shimojō Village has an unknown founding date, but it is an ancient shrine predating the compilation of the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}} in Engi 5 (927), and is presumed to have originated between the late Nara and early Heian periods. There are several theories about its original location, and because primary historical sources are lacking, it cannot be determined with certainty. It is believed to have been moved and then enshrined at its present site. In the early Muromachi period, the Shimajō clan, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114}}, came to {{ill|lt=Ina District|Q11380911|WD=Q11380911}}. In the Bunmei era they built a castle at Yoshioka in Shimojō Village and made the already-enshrined Hachiman-gū their clan shrine, renaming the place Chinzeino. In Eishō 4 (1507), lord Shimajō Ieuji had shrine buildings for Hachiman-sha and Suwa-sha constructed by temple carpenter Yoshimura Izumi from Kyoto. The Shimajō clan later fell at the end of Tenshō, but local devotion to this shrine remained strong and it was crowded with worshippers. In Keian 2 (1649), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481}} issued a red-seal grant of 10 koku to "Chinzeino Village Hachiman-gū," and successive shoguns continued issuing renewed red-seal documents through the end of the shogunate. In Genbun 6 (1741), priest Chinzei Kiyoyuki transferred {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} from Suga no Ne-no-jinja and established this as Oyamada Shrine, enshrining him as main deity and Hachiman and Suwa as side deities; this arrangement was approved by Yoshida household official Urabe Kaneyū. In Enkyō 2 (1749), records state Oyamada Shrine buildings were rebuilt by carpenter Suzuki Kurōbei Fujiwara Shigematsu. A surviving ridge tag also states that in Hōreki 8 (1758) the Hachirō Myōjin hall was built. Hachirō Myōjin refers to the spirit shrine of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356|lt=Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo}}, ancestor of the Chinzei priestly family. It is inferred that sometime in the mid-Edo period, due to some change, Hachirō Myōjin replaced Suwa Myōjin in the former Eishō-era Suwa hall. In 1872 (Meiji 5), it became a gōsha. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the Hachiman-sha hall and Suwa-sha hall built in the Eishō era (the latter now changed to Hachirō Myōjin) were designated National Important Cultural Properties. The shrine area is one of Nagano's "100 Natural Areas" and has around 280 species of both southern and northern limit plants, making it botanically valuable. === Precincts === * Torii * Stone steps * Haiden * Sacred trees === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings - In 1912 the Ōjin Tennō-sha and Tametomo-sha were each designated as Important Cultural Properties (then Special Protected Buildings). In 1955 they were consolidated as "Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings".Agency for Cultural Affairs Notice No. 5, 2 February 1955 ** Side sanctuary Ōjin Tennō-sha honden ** Side sanctuary Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo-sha honden === Images === ファイル:大山田神社 鳥居２.jpg|Oyamada Shrine Torii 2, at the top of the long stone stairway ファイル:大山田神社 拝殿.jpg|Oyamada Shrine haiden and covered honden, with temizuya in front ファイル:大山田神社 神木.jpg|Oyamada Shrine sacred tree, giant cedar === Address === 4588 Hisawa, Shimojō, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === {{参照方法|date=December 2020}} * ''Shimoina History, Vol. 4'', Shimoina History Compilation Association, rep. {{ill|lt=Shiohito Ichimura|Q56253217|WD=Q56253217}}, published by {{ill|lt=Shinano Education Society|Q11386177|WD=Q11386177}}, 1961. * ''Shinshū Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'', {{ill|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|Q11386181|WD=Q11386181|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun Co.}}, 1981. === External links === * [https://shimojo-kanko.jp/miru/ooyamadajinjya Oyamada Shrine] - Shimojō Village Tourism Association {{commonscat|Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=長野県}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやまたしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shinano Province]] [[Category:長野県の神社]] [[Category:長野県の旧郷社]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:下條村の建築物]] [[Category:下條村の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Commons category (P373) == * Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '長野県下伊那郡下條村陽皐', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} [[JA:大山田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q85878781}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Oyamada Shrine |画像 = [[File:大山田神社 鳥居１.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}{{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}{{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}Hisawa 4588 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}、former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |例祭 = Second Sunday of April, second Sunday of October |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano|label=Oyamada Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oyamada Shrine'''（おおやまだじんじゃ）is a shrine located in {{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}, {{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}, {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === * Main sanctuary: {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}}（Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto） * Side sanctuary: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}（Hachiman-sha） * Side sanctuary: Hachirō Myōjin（{{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356}}）; before mid-Edo this was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=q|WD=Q9150391}}（Suwa-sha） === History === Oyamada Shrine in Hisawa, Shimojō Village has an unknown founding date, but it is an ancient shrine predating the compilation of the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}} in Engi 5 (927), and is presumed to have originated between the late Nara and early Heian periods. There are several theories about its original location, and because primary historical sources are lacking, it cannot be determined with certainty. It is believed to have been moved and then enshrined at its present site. In the early Muromachi period, the Shimajō clan, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114}}, came to {{ill|lt=Ina District|Q11380911|WD=Q11380911}}. In the Bunmei era they built a castle at Yoshioka in Shimojō Village and made the already-enshrined Hachiman-gū their clan shrine, renaming the place Chinzeino. In Eishō 4 (1507), lord Shimajō Ieuji had shrine buildings for Hachiman-sha and Suwa-sha constructed by temple carpenter Yoshimura Izumi from Kyoto. The Shimajō clan later fell at the end of Tenshō, but local devotion to this shrine remained strong and it was crowded with worshippers. In Keian 2 (1649), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481}} issued a red-seal grant of 10 koku to "Chinzeino Village Hachiman-gū," and successive shoguns continued issuing renewed red-seal documents through the end of the shogunate. In Genbun 6 (1741), priest Chinzei Kiyoyuki transferred {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q276944}} from Suga no Ne-no-jinja and established this as Oyamada Shrine, enshrining him as main deity and Hachiman and Suwa as side deities; this arrangement was approved by Yoshida household official Urabe Kaneyū. In Enkyō 2 (1749), records state Oyamada Shrine buildings were rebuilt by carpenter Suzuki Kurōbei Fujiwara Shigematsu. A surviving ridge tag also states that in Hōreki 8 (1758) the Hachirō Myōjin hall was built. Hachirō Myōjin refers to the spirit shrine of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356|lt=Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo}}, ancestor of the Chinzei priestly family. It is inferred that sometime in the mid-Edo period, due to some change, Hachirō Myōjin replaced Suwa Myōjin in the former Eishō-era Suwa hall. In 1872 (Meiji 5), it became a gōsha. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the Hachiman-sha hall and Suwa-sha hall built in the Eishō era (the latter now changed to Hachirō Myōjin) were designated National Important Cultural Properties. The shrine area is one of Nagano's "100 Natural Areas" and has around 280 species of both southern and northern limit plants, making it botanically valuable. === Precincts === * Torii * Stone steps * Haiden * Sacred trees === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings - In 1912 the Ōjin Tennō-sha and Tametomo-sha were each designated as Important Cultural Properties (then Special Protected Buildings). In 1955 they were consolidated as "Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings".Agency for Cultural Affairs Notice No. 5, 2 February 1955 ** Side sanctuary Ōjin Tennō-sha honden ** Side sanctuary Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo-sha honden === Images === ファイル:大山田神社 鳥居２.jpg|Oyamada Shrine Torii 2, at the top of the long stone stairway ファイル:大山田神社 拝殿.jpg|Oyamada Shrine haiden and covered honden, with temizuya in front ファイル:大山田神社 神木.jpg|Oyamada Shrine sacred tree, giant cedar === Address === 4588 Hisawa, Shimojō, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === {{参照方法|date=December 2020}} * ''Shimoina History, Vol. 4'', Shimoina History Compilation Association, rep. {{ill|lt=Shiohito Ichimura|Q56253217|WD=Q56253217}}, published by {{ill|lt=Shinano Education Society|Q11386177|WD=Q11386177}}, 1961. * ''Shinshū Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'', {{ill|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|Q11386181|WD=Q11386181|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun Co.}}, 1981. === External links === * [https://shimojo-kanko.jp/miru/ooyamadajinjya Oyamada Shrine] - Shimojō Village Tourism Association {{commonscat|Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=長野県}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやまたしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shinano Province]] [[Category:長野県の神社]] [[Category:長野県の旧郷社]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:下條村の建築物]] [[Category:下條村の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

[[JA:大山田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q85878781}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Oyamada Shrine |画像 = [[File:大山田神社 鳥居１.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}{{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}{{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}Hisawa 4588 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}、former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |例祭 = Second Sunday of April, second Sunday of October |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano|label=Oyamada Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oyamada Shrine'''（おおやまだじんじゃ）is a shrine located in {{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}, {{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}, {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === * Main sanctuary: {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}}（Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto） * Side sanctuary: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}（Hachiman-sha） * Side sanctuary: Hachirō Myōjin（{{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356}}）; before mid-Edo this was {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=q|WD=Q9150391}}（Suwa-sha） === History === Oyamada Shrine in Hisawa, Shimojō Village has an unknown founding date, but it is an ancient shrine predating the compilation of the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}} in Engi 5 (927), and is presumed to have originated between the late Nara and early Heian periods. There are several theories about its original location, and because primary historical sources are lacking, it cannot be determined with certainty. It is believed to have been moved and then enshrined at its present site. In the early Muromachi period, the Shimajō clan, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114}}, came to {{ill|lt=Ina District|Q11380911|WD=Q11380911}}. In the Bunmei era they built a castle at Yoshioka in Shimojō Village and made the already-enshrined Hachiman-gū their clan shrine, renaming the place Chinzeino. In Eishō 4 (1507), lord Shimajō Ieuji had shrine buildings for Hachiman-sha and Suwa-sha constructed by temple carpenter Yoshimura Izumi from Kyoto. The Shimajō clan later fell at the end of Tenshō, but local devotion to this shrine remained strong and it was crowded with worshippers. In Keian 2 (1649), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481}} issued a red-seal grant of 10 koku to "Chinzeino Village Hachiman-gū," and successive shoguns continued issuing renewed red-seal documents through the end of the shogunate. In Genbun 6 (1741), priest Chinzei Kiyoyuki transferred {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q276944}} from Suga no Ne-no-jinja and established this as Oyamada Shrine, enshrining him as main deity and Hachiman and Suwa as side deities; this arrangement was approved by Yoshida household official Urabe Kaneyū. In Enkyō 2 (1749), records state Oyamada Shrine buildings were rebuilt by carpenter Suzuki Kurōbei Fujiwara Shigematsu. A surviving ridge tag also states that in Hōreki 8 (1758) the Hachirō Myōjin hall was built. Hachirō Myōjin refers to the spirit shrine of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356|lt=Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo}}, ancestor of the Chinzei priestly family. It is inferred that sometime in the mid-Edo period, due to some change, Hachirō Myōjin replaced Suwa Myōjin in the former Eishō-era Suwa hall. In 1872 (Meiji 5), it became a gōsha. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the Hachiman-sha hall and Suwa-sha hall built in the Eishō era (the latter now changed to Hachirō Myōjin) were designated National Important Cultural Properties. The shrine area is one of Nagano's "100 Natural Areas" and has around 280 species of both southern and northern limit plants, making it botanically valuable. === Precincts === * Torii * Stone steps * Haiden * Sacred trees === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings - In 1912 the Ōjin Tennō-sha and Tametomo-sha were each designated as Important Cultural Properties (then Special Protected Buildings). In 1955 they were consolidated as "Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings".Agency for Cultural Affairs Notice No. 5, 2 February 1955 ** Side sanctuary Ōjin Tennō-sha honden ** Side sanctuary Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo-sha honden === Images === ファイル:大山田神社 鳥居２.jpg|Oyamada Shrine Torii 2, at the top of the long stone stairway ファイル:大山田神社 拝殿.jpg|Oyamada Shrine haiden and covered honden, with temizuya in front ファイル:大山田神社 神木.jpg|Oyamada Shrine sacred tree, giant cedar === Address === 4588 Hisawa, Shimojō, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === {{参照方法|date=December 2020}} * ''Shimoina History, Vol. 4'', Shimoina History Compilation Association, rep. {{ill|lt=Shiohito Ichimura|Q56253217|WD=Q56253217}}, published by {{ill|lt=Shinano Education Society|Q11386177|WD=Q11386177}}, 1961. * ''Shinshū Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'', {{ill|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|Q11386181|WD=Q11386181|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun Co.}}, 1981. === External links === * [https://shimojo-kanko.jp/miru/ooyamadajinjya Oyamada Shrine] - Shimojō Village Tourism Association {{commonscat|Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=長野県}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやまたしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shinano Province]] [[Category:長野県の神社]] [[Category:長野県の旧郷社]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:下條村の建築物]] [[Category:下條村の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

[[JA:大山田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q85878781}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Oyamada Shrine |画像 = [[File:大山田神社 鳥居１.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}{{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}{{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}Hisawa 4588 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}、former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |例祭 = Second Sunday of April, second Sunday of October |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagano|label=Oyamada Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oyamada Shrine'''（おおやまだじんじゃ）is a shrine located in {{ill|lt=Shimojō|Q1203050|WD=Q1203050}}, {{ill|lt=Shimoina District|Q726999|WD=Q726999}}, {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === * Main sanctuary: {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}}（Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto） * Side sanctuary: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}（Hachiman-sha） * Side sanctuary: Hachirō Myōjin（{{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356}}）; before mid-Edo this was {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=q|WD=Q9150391}}（Suwa-sha） === History === Oyamada Shrine in Hisawa, Shimojō Village has an unknown founding date, but it is an ancient shrine predating the compilation of the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}} in Engi 5 (927), and is presumed to have originated between the late Nara and early Heian periods. There are several theories about its original location, and because primary historical sources are lacking, it cannot be determined with certainty. It is believed to have been moved and then enshrined at its present site. In the early Muromachi period, the Shimajō clan, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114}}, came to {{ill|lt=Ina District|Q11380911|WD=Q11380911}}. In the Bunmei era they built a castle at Yoshioka in Shimojō Village and made the already-enshrined Hachiman-gū their clan shrine, renaming the place Chinzeino. In Eishō 4 (1507), lord Shimajō Ieuji had shrine buildings for Hachiman-sha and Suwa-sha constructed by temple carpenter Yoshimura Izumi from Kyoto. The Shimajō clan later fell at the end of Tenshō, but local devotion to this shrine remained strong and it was crowded with worshippers. In Keian 2 (1649), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481}} issued a red-seal grant of 10 koku to "Chinzeino Village Hachiman-gū," and successive shoguns continued issuing renewed red-seal documents through the end of the shogunate. In Genbun 6 (1741), priest Chinzei Kiyoyuki transferred {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q276944}} from Suga no Ne-no-jinja and established this as Oyamada Shrine, enshrining him as main deity and Hachiman and Suwa as side deities; this arrangement was approved by Yoshida household official Urabe Kaneyū. In Enkyō 2 (1749), records state Oyamada Shrine buildings were rebuilt by carpenter Suzuki Kurōbei Fujiwara Shigematsu. A surviving ridge tag also states that in Hōreki 8 (1758) the Hachirō Myōjin hall was built. Hachirō Myōjin refers to the spirit shrine of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|Q1339356|WD=Q1339356|lt=Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo}}, ancestor of the Chinzei priestly family. It is inferred that sometime in the mid-Edo period, due to some change, Hachirō Myōjin replaced Suwa Myōjin in the former Eishō-era Suwa hall. In 1872 (Meiji 5), it became a gōsha. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the Hachiman-sha hall and Suwa-sha hall built in the Eishō era (the latter now changed to Hachirō Myōjin) were designated National Important Cultural Properties. The shrine area is one of Nagano's "100 Natural Areas" and has around 280 species of both southern and northern limit plants, making it botanically valuable. === Precincts === * Torii * Stone steps * Haiden * Sacred trees === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties === * Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings - In 1912 the Ōjin Tennō-sha and Tametomo-sha were each designated as Important Cultural Properties (then Special Protected Buildings). In 1955 they were consolidated as "Oyamada Shrine, 2 buildings".Agency for Cultural Affairs Notice No. 5, 2 February 1955 ** Side sanctuary Ōjin Tennō-sha honden ** Side sanctuary Chinzei Hachirō Tametomo-sha honden === Images === ファイル:大山田神社 鳥居２.jpg|Oyamada Shrine Torii 2, at the top of the long stone stairway ファイル:大山田神社 拝殿.jpg|Oyamada Shrine haiden and covered honden, with temizuya in front ファイル:大山田神社 神木.jpg|Oyamada Shrine sacred tree, giant cedar === Address === 4588 Hisawa, Shimojō, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === {{参照方法|date=December 2020}} * ''Shimoina History, Vol. 4'', Shimoina History Compilation Association, rep. {{ill|lt=Shiohito Ichimura|Q56253217|WD=Q56253217}}, published by {{ill|lt=Shinano Education Society|Q11386177|WD=Q11386177}}, 1961. * ''Shinshū Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'', {{ill|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|Q11386181|WD=Q11386181|lt=Shinano Mainichi Shimbun Co.}}, 1981. === External links === * [https://shimojo-kanko.jp/miru/ooyamadajinjya Oyamada Shrine] - Shimojō Village Tourism Association {{commonscat|Oyamada shrine (Shimojo, Nagano)}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=長野県}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやまたしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyamada Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shinano Province]] [[Category:長野県の神社]] [[Category:長野県の旧郷社]] [[Category:長野県の重要文化財]] [[Category:下條村の建築物]] [[Category:下條村の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]
